title_en: Using Preprint Sources in Science News: Do Transparent Disclosures Enhance Credibility?
title_zh: Using Preprint Sources in Science News: Do Transparent Disclosures Enhance Credibility?
abstract_en: This paper tests how audiences react when science news stories cite preprints that have not yet undergone peer review. Transparent disclosure about the status of the source improves perceptions of honesty and does not necessarily reduce perceived usefulness, especially when readers are told why the finding still matters.
abstract_zh: This paper tests how audiences react when science news stories cite preprints that have not yet undergone peer review. Transparent disclosure about the status of the source improves perceptions of honesty and does not necessarily reduce perceived usefulness, especially when readers are told why the finding still matters.
plain_summary_zh: This paper asks whether science news becomes more credible when journalists clearly disclose that a source is a preprint rather than a peer-reviewed article. The results suggest that transparent labeling usually protects trust better than treating preprints like finalized science.
plain_summary_en: This paper asks whether science news becomes more credible when journalists clearly disclose that a source is a preprint rather than a peer-reviewed article. The results suggest that transparent labeling usually protects trust better than treating preprints like finalized science.
research_direction_zh: It contributes to research on transparency in science journalism, especially how newsrooms balance speed with credibility.
research_direction_en: It contributes to research on transparency in science journalism, especially how newsrooms balance speed with credibility.
raw_title: Using Preprint Sources in Science News: Do Transparent Disclosures Enhance Credibility?
raw_abstract: This paper tests how audiences react when science news stories cite preprints that have not yet undergone peer review. Transparent disclosure about the status of the source improves perceptions of honesty and does not necessarily reduce perceived usefulness, especially when readers are told why the finding still matters.
